attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of generalised fatigue and pruritus. He mentions that he has been looking "a bit yellow" lately. He has a medical history of ulcerative colitis diagnosed five years ago, currently treated with infliximab infusions once every eight weeks and twice daily mesalazine tablets. He has no other significant medical history and does not smoke.

On examination, he appears clinically jaundiced with visible scratch marks on his trunk and upper extremities. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant. Liver function tests show:

  • Bilirubin: 48 µmol/L (2-24)

  • ALP: 465 U/L (30-110)

  • GGT: 245 U/L (0-60)

  • AST: 78 U/L (0-45)

  • ALT: 97 U/L (0-55)

Abdominal ultrasound shows diffuse thickening of the walls of the common bile ducts and areas of focal duct dilatation. The doctor informs him that he is now at an elevated risk of developing certain malignancies and that some of these can be screened for on an ongoing basis.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate additional screening test to recommend?
options: Annual CT chest, abdomen and pelvis
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), a chronic liver disease characterised by progressive inflammation and fibrosis of the bile ducts. The combination of fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, abnormal liver function tests (particularly elevated ALP and GGT), and ultrasound findings strongly suggest this diagnosis. PSC is closely associated with inflammatory bowel disease, particularly ulcerative colitis, which this patient has.

Annual colonoscopy is the most appropriate additional screening test to recommend. Patients with both PSC and ulcerative colitis have a significantly increased risk of developing colorectal cancer compared to those with ulcerative colitis alone. The lifetime risk of colorectal cancer in patients with both conditions can be as high as 31%. Due to this elevated risk, annual colonoscopy is recommended for colorectal cancer surveillance in these patients, starting from the time of PSC diagnosis.

Annual endoscopy is not routinely recommended for patients with PSC. While there may be a slightly increased risk of upper gastrointestinal cancers in PSC patients, the evidence does not support annual upper endoscopy as a screening tool.

Annual MRCP (Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography) can be useful in monitoring the progression of PSC and detecting biliary strictures. However, it is not typically recommended as an annual screening test for malignancy. While PSC patients have an increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma, there is no consensus on the effectiveness of routine MRCP for early detection of this cancer.

Annual CT chest, abdomen and pelvis would expose the patient to unnecessary radiation and is not recommended as a routine screening test in PSC. The risks associated with repeated CT scans outweigh the potential benefits in this context.

Annual AFP (Alpha-Fetoprotein) testing is sometimes used in the surveillance of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in patients with cirrhosis. However, AFP alone is not a reliable screening tool for HCC, and routine annual AFP testing is not recommended for all PSC patients, especially in the absence of cirrhosis.

In addition to annual colonoscopy, management of this patient should include addressing the underlying PSC, managing symptoms such as pruritus, and considering ursodeoxycholic acid therapy. Regular monitoring of liver function and periodic imaging of the biliary tree are also important aspects of care for PSC patients.
learningPoints: The risk of developing colorectal cancer is significantly increased in patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis and ulcerative colitis.
Annual colonoscopy is recommended for colorectal cancer screening in patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis and ulcerative colitis.
references: Ananthakrishnan AN, Cagan A, Gainer VS, Su-Chun C, Cai T, Szolovits P, et al. Mortality and extraintestinal cancers in patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis and inflammatory bowel disease. Journal of Crohn's and Colitis. 2014 Sep;8(9):956-63. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4136996/

Chapman MH, Thorburn D, Hirschfield GM, Webster GG, Rushbrook SM, Alexander G, et al. British Society of Gastroenterology and UK-PSC guidelines for the diagnosis and management of primary sclerosing cholangitis. Gut. 2016 Jun;68(8):1356-78. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31154395/

Fung BM, Lindor KD, Tabibian JH. Cancer risk in primary sclerosing cholangitis: Epidemiology, prevention, and surveillance strategies. World J Gastroenterol. 2019 Feb;25(6):659-71. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30783370/

Satiya J, Mousa OY, Gupta K, Trivedi S, Oman SP, Wijarnpreecha K, et al. Diagnostic yield of magnetic resonance imaging for cholangiocarcinoma in primary sclerosing cholangitis: a meta-analysis. Clin Exp Hepatol. 2020 Feb;6(1):35–41. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7062114/
